meta.title: The Midnight Coder - Technical Blog
meta.description: Deep technical writing on software engineering, distributed systems, and the craft of building systems that scale with modern responsive layouts

sections.headline: BUILDING SYSTEMS THAT SCALE.
sections.subheadline: Deep technical writing on software engineering, distributed systems, and the craft of writing code that lasts. No fluff, no tutorials, no clickbait. Just technical depth from 15 years of building production systems at scale. Real problems, real solutions, real metrics.

sections.heading: ABOUT THIS BLOG
sections.body:
I've been building software for 15 years, from embedded systems to distributed platforms. This blog is where I write about what I learn—architecture decisions that worked (and didn't), performance optimizations that surprised me, and opinions on the state of our industry.

No fluff, no tutorials, no clickbait. Just technical depth. If you're looking for how to build a to-do app in React, this isn't the place. If you want to understand why your microservices architecture is costing you $200K/month in infrastructure, read on. Every article includes real metrics, real code, and real lessons from production.

items.title: WHY I STOPPED USING MICROSERVICES
items.description: After 5 years of microservices architecture at scale, we migrated back to a modular monolith. Here's why, and the $200K/year infrastructure savings we achieved. Includes real metrics and migration strategy.

items.title: THE HIDDEN COST OF ABSTRACTIONS
items.description: Every layer of abstraction adds complexity. When does the cost outweigh the benefit? A deep dive into framework fatigue and the return to fundamentals. Case studies from 3 major rewrites.

items.title: BUILDING A DATABASE FROM SCRATCH
items.description: A series documenting the journey of building a simple KV store in Rust, from basic storage to distributed consensus. Part 1 of 5. Complete with benchmarks and production considerations.

items.title: POSTGRESQL AT 10M QPS
items.description: How we scaled PostgreSQL to handle 10 million queries per second without sharding. Connection pooling, caching strategies, and query optimization. Includes configuration and monitoring setup.

items.title: THE DEATH OF THE SENIOR ENGINEER
items.description: Why the industry's obsession with seniority titles is broken, and what we should measure instead. Hint: it's not years of experience. Proposes a new competency framework.

items.title: RUST VS GO FOR BACKEND SERVICES
items.description: A practical comparison after building the same service in both languages. Performance, developer experience, and ecosystem maturity. Includes benchmarks and production deployment costs.

items.title: THE END OF CLOUD NATIVE
items.description: Why the cloud native movement has gone too far, and when you should choose a monolith over Kubernetes. Spoiler: more often than you think. Decision framework included.

items.title: ZERO DOWNTIME DEPLOYMENTS
items.description: A comprehensive guide to deploying without downtime. Blue-green deployments, canary releases, and database migrations that don't break things. Includes infrastructure-as-code examples.

items.quote: Your article on microservices convinced our team to reconsider our architecture. We saved $200K/year in infrastructure costs after the migration. The ROI was immediate.
items.name: Sarah Kim

items.quote: The database series is the best technical writing I've read in years. Clear, practical, and deep without being academic. I've implemented several patterns from it.
items.name: Marcus Johnson

items.quote: The PostgreSQL scaling article helped us go from 1M to 10M QPS without sharding. The connection pooling strategy alone was worth the read.
items.name: Jennifer Wu

items.quote: Finally, someone writing about the reality of senior engineering instead of the fantasy. Your article on seniority titles sparked real conversations at my company.
items.name: David Park

sections.heading: GET IN TOUCH
sections.subheading: Open to consulting, speaking, and interesting conversations about distributed systems. No sales pitches, just technical discussions.
